html:
Laurentius Petri Nericius (a Latinization of the Swedish name Lars Petersson from Närke), born in Örebro in 1499 and died on October 26, 1573, in Uppsala, was a Swedish clergyman who became Sweden's first Evangelical Lutheran Archbishop from 1531 to 1573. He is also known as Laurentius Petri the Elder, to distinguish him from his son-in-law, Laurentius Petri Gothus.

He was elected Archbishop of the Diocese of Uppsala in August 1531 and ordained by the Bishop of the Diocese of Västerås, Peder Månsson (Petrus Magni). The Church of Sweden maintains that this event represents a continuity through the Reformation, preserving the apostolic succession.

== Biography ==

Laurentius Petri was the brother of the Swedish reformer Olaus Petri, and the son of the blacksmith Peter Olofsson and Kristina Larsdotter. Through the consecration of Laurentius Petri as bishop by Petrus Magni, the Swedish bishops maintained an unbroken apostolic succession, something that is disputed by the Roman Catholic Church (most recently, although not explicitly, in the document Dominus Iesus from 2000).

Laurentius Petri's early life is shrouded in mystery. The first time his name is definitively mentioned is in 1527, when he was sent to study at German universities, including Wittenberg University, at the expense of Gustav Vasa, although he likely had already been studying there.

After returning home, he became a schoolmaster in Uppsala. He was then elected at the Church Council in Stockholm in 1531, likely under pressure from Gustav Vasa, as Archbishop of the Diocese of Uppsala and thus of the Swedish ecclesiastical province. Laurentius Petri was more cautious than his brother and retreated from the king's demands when they became too great. During the first few years, his time was mainly devoted to the new Bible translation, which was published in 1541.

He made his first significant contribution to church affairs with his "Explanation of the Västerås Recess and Ordinant," written in 1533, where he developed his ecclesiastical political and organizational program in contrast to the king's approach to implementing the recess. As early as 1533, Gustav Vasa limited the archbishop's authority through a so-called union, and this was further reduced during the German period, when the king's chronic distrust and desire to dissolve the episcopal institution put Laurentius Petri's compliance and resilience to the test.

He was even forced to sign the death sentence against his own brother, Olaus Petri, and Laurentius Andreæ, as a co-signer in 1540. For more than a decade, Laurentius Petri found himself sidelined, after the leadership of the church was handed over to Georg Norman as superintendent in 1539. In silence, he worked for a return to the nationally organized church, carried out the important revision of the church books (the hymnal and the mass book), and continued the work on a church order that he had begun during his first term in office.

With Georg Norman's death in 1553, Laurentius Petri was once again at the center of church leadership. During this time, he developed an extensive literary output, which dominated Swedish printing for 20 years, although many of his writings were not published until after his death. Several of them remain unpublished. In 1555, he published a comprehensive postilla, and in 1557, Sweden's first treatise on temperance: "Emoot dryckenskap." His relationship with the king was now relatively good, although conflicts did occur. One such conflict was Gustav Vasa's marriage in 1552 to his deceased wife's niece, Katarina Stenbock, which Laurentius Petri opposed as an incestuous union. However, the "Svenska Krönika" that Laurentius Petri completed in 1559 did receive the king's approval. He was also commissioned by the king for diplomatic missions, including leading a peace delegation to Russia in 1557.

During Erik XIV's reign, Laurentius Petri gained more freedom. The church order proposal that he had been prevented from presenting by Gustav Vasa could now be presented at the Riksdag (national assembly) in Stockholm in 1562, and although the king did not approve it, Laurentius Petri was able to work for its practical implementation. For a few years, his interest was largely focused on the confessional polemics against Dionysius Beurraeus and Calvinism, which he combated in a series of writings, including "Om någor stycker wårs Herras Jesu Christi nattward anrörandes," where the new German-Lutheran orthodoxy is clearly evident, and the Swedish church is explicitly designated as Lutheran for the first time. Laurentius Petri's activities and interests encompassed all aspects of church life, with pastoral care being central. He oversaw new editions of the church books (the hymnal in 1567 with additions in 1572 and the first catechism edition in 1567) and worked to improve church music, among other things.

In the political conflicts of the time, he showed his adaptability to secular power. He condemned Duke Johan in 1563 and remained loyal to Erik XIV for a long time, but in 1568 he joined the dukes and later condemned the former king not only to lose the crown but also to death. Sick and elderly, he saw his life's work crowned with victory when the church order drafted by Laurentius Petri himself was adopted in 1571.

How can we be sure about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well-documented in archives. The uncertainty lies more in interpretation and how later generations perceive them – especially since those in power themselves played a role in shaping historical narratives.
tldr: Laurentius Petri was a significant Swedish clergyman who became the first archbishop of the newly established Lutheran Church in the 16th century. He dedicated much of his work to writing books, translating the Bible, and establishing rules for how the church should operate.
lattlast:
Laurentius Petri was born in Örebro in 1499 and became a highly influential leader within the Swedish church. He was appointed Archbishop of Uppsala in 1531, making him the highest-ranking leader of the church after Sweden transitioned from Catholicism to Lutheranism. During his tenure, he worked diligently to create new books and regulations for religious services.

He was a prolific writer, authoring works such as a book on sobriety in 1557, as well as several other important texts for the church. He was also involved in political matters and was sent by the king on diplomatic missions to other countries, such as Russia. Despite sometimes having conflicts with the kings he served, he managed to implement many significant changes.

Towards the end of his life, he dedicated much of his time to music and education for believers. He wrote a new hymn book and a catechism, which is a concise summary of Christian teachings. His work laid the foundation for how the Swedish church would function for a long time afterward.
